one_line_takeaway: Overparameterized (wide, memorizing) DNNs are not just easier to attack but also easier to unlearn: with per-width validation tuning, existing unlearning methods achieve much better privacy/bias-removal-vs-utility tradeoffs on wide models by editing decision regions locally, provided (for bias removal) the method actually uses the forget set.
key_finding: Unlearning is markedly more effective on overparameterized models: they sit closer to the ideal utility-vs-goal corner and achieve high privacy/bias-removal gains at reasonable utility cost, whereas underparameterized models are restricted to low-gain tradeoffs. A nuance the authors stress: some underparameterized models show 'good' unlearning privacy only because their original model already had good privacy (low forget-vs-forget-test loss gap), so the method barely changes them, i.e., seemingly-succeeding with near-zero privacy gain and low utility loss, unlike overparameterized models whose perfect-fit forget loss near zero forces active change. Successful bias removal on overparameterized models specifically requires a forget-set-using method: L1-sparsity, which only uses the retain set, fails at bias removal. Decision-region analysis explains the benefit: overparameterization enables delicate, local edits (high change near forgotten examples, high similarity far away).
core_contribution: An empirical study of how a DNN's parameterization level (implemented as network width scale) affects machine unlearning. The authors' OWN contribution is threefold: (1) they define a validation-based hyperparameter-tuning procedure with two new goal-specific validation objectives (one for data-privacy unlearning, one for bias-removal unlearning) that retunes each existing unlearning method per width/goal/dataset; (2) using this, they show empirically that unlearning generally works better on overparameterized models, improving privacy/bias-removal at reasonable utility cost, with the caveat that bias removal requires a method that actually uses the forget set; (3) they adapt the decision-region similarity score of Somepalli et al. [15] into a 'far' similarity score plus a 'proximity' change score to show that overparameterization lets unlearning modify decision regions locally around forgotten examples while leaving the rest of the function intact. The paper does NOT propose a new unlearning algorithm; the five unlearning methods (SCRUB, NegGrad+, L1-sparsity, SalUn, RL) are all prior work that it benchmarks.
threat_model: The privacy half of the paper assumes a loss-based membership inference attacker: an adversary that, given an example, uses the model's cross-entropy loss on that example to decide whether it was a (formerly) training/forget-set member versus an unseen test example from the same classes. The paper operationalizes this attacker as a logistic-regression classifier trained on the single loss feature with 10-fold stratified cross-validation; loss features clipped to [-100,100]. An MIA accuracy of 0.5 (random guessing) is ideal privacy; larger |MIA - 0.5| means the unlearned examples remain distinguishable and privacy leaks. The motivating adversarial context is GDPR 'right to be forgotten' and the fact that overparameterized DNNs memorize training data (raising extraction and MIA risk). The bias-removal half is not adversarial; there the 'goal' is to raise forget-set error so the model no longer reproduces labels induced by biasing examples.
limitations: Purely empirical and observational; no theory and no new algorithm, only retuning of existing methods (the authors themselves suggest future methods should build parameterization/goal awareness into their core). Scope is small-image classification only (CIFAR-10 32x32, Tiny ImageNet resized to 32x32) with ResNet-18/34 and one 3-layer MLP; no NLP, generative, or large-scale models. Only 3 random seeds per configuration. The MIA is a single, relatively weak loss-threshold attack (logistic regression on one loss feature), so 'privacy' conclusions may not transfer to stronger MIAs (e.g., LiRA-style). Parameterization is varied purely by width; depth and other capacity axes are untested. Decision-region metrics depend on a hand-set delta (results shown only for delta=10; the 0.3/0.5 markers in the Fig. 4 bias-removal panels are lambda balance settings, not delta) and on 2D plane sampling, which may not capture high-dimensional behavior.

datasets_used: Tiny ImageNet (200 classes, 100,000 train images, resized to 32x32x3)
quantitative_results: 5 experimental settings: ResNet-34/Tiny ImageNet/1000 forget from 20 classes; ResNet-18/Tiny ImageNet/500 forget from 10 classes; ResNet-18/CIFAR-10/200 forget from 2 classes; ResNet-18/CIFAR-10/600 forget from 3 classes; 3-layer FC/CIFAR-10/200 forget from 2 classes
21 distinct model widths per ResNet setting, 13 widths for the FC network, 3 random seeds per configuration; width scale range [0.02, 1]
Total of 64,656 individual unlearning runs across all methods/widths/seeds
519 models trained from scratch: 228 original (pre-unlearning) models + 291 retrained-from-scratch reference models
Overparameterization threshold: original avg train error < 0.1% (>=99.9% memorization); underparameterization threshold: avg train error > 10%
Decision-region analysis: m=300 planes each with a 2500-point uniform grid, similarity/change reported at delta=10 (Fig. 4, ResNet-18/Tiny ImageNet, 500 forget examples)
MIA implemented as logistic regression on the single CE-loss feature (clipped to [-100,100]) with 10-fold stratified cross-validation; ideal MIA score = 0.5

lambda (goal-vs-utility balance) values: privacy lambda in {0.1,0.2} (or {0.1,0.3} for CIFAR-10 200-forget); bias removal lambda in {0.3,0.5}; category/relative plots use lambda=0.1 (privacy), 0.3 (bias)
baselines_compared: L1-sparsity unlearning [12] (retain-set-only, l1 pruning; fails bias removal)
Random Labeling / RL [14] (random-label fine-tuning, all params)
Retrain-from-scratch (gold-standard reference) and the original pre-unlearning model
